# URL Adapter (v1)

**Date:** 2026-04-25
**Scope:** `internal/adapters/url/` (new); precursor work in `internal/adapters/plex/` (cross-adapter `OnStop` cleanup; see §"Plex precursor")
**Author:** generated by brainstorming session
**Status:** design — pending implementation plan

## Problem

The bridge currently has one cast-source adapter (Plex). The original v1
design (`docs/specs/2026-04-19-mister-groovy-relay-design.md` §4.5)
explicitly recommended the URL adapter as the *second* adapter, ahead of
Jellyfin, because:

> The URL-input adapter is recommended as the *second* adapter
> specifically because it exercises the `internal/core/` boundary
> against a maximally-different shape from Plex — confirming the
> boundary is right before Jellyfin (which is structurally similar to
> Plex) arrives.

Plex is a *cast target* with LAN advertisement, companion HTTP, server
registration, and timeline broadcasting. Jellyfin is structurally a
near-twin. A URL adapter has none of those — it is a *URL acceptor*: a
human pastes a URL or a script POSTs one, the bridge plays it. If
`core.Manager` cleanly absorbs both Plex's cast-target shape and the
URL acceptor shape with no leakage in either direction, the
`adapters.Adapter` boundary is the right one. If it doesn't, the work
to make it right is cheaper to do now (with two adapters) than later
(with three or four).

This spec defines the minimum-viable URL adapter that maximally probes
the abstraction.

## Goals

- Add a second adapter (`adapters.url`) that accepts an HTTP/HTTPS
  media URL and casts it through the existing data plane.
- Validate that `core.Manager` is genuinely adapter-agnostic. In
  particular: (a) an adapter with no upstream protocol, no goroutines,
  and no LAN advertisement registers and lifecycles cleanly; (b)
  cross-adapter session preemption (URL POST while Plex casting) tears
  the prior session down and notifies the prior adapter via `OnStop`,
  *and* the prior adapter's `OnStop` body actually does enough cleanup
  for the prior controller to learn the session has stopped — see
  §"Plex precursor" for the work this surfaces.
- Ship a *usable* surface — settings-UI form + JSON HTTP API — without
  stretching scope to bookmarks, history, or yt-dlp.

## Non-Goals

- Bookmarks, history, or saved-URL list.
- yt-dlp / YouTube / Internet Archive page scraping. (URL must be
  directly ingestible by ffmpeg.)
- `rtsp://`, `rtmp://`, `file://` schemes. v1 supports `http://` and
  `https://` only.
- Per-session pause / seek / stop UI controls. Sessions are
  fire-and-forget: they end on EOF, on preempt by another POST, or via
  the existing bridge-wide stop mechanism.
- A cross-adapter "Now Playing" sidebar panel. That belongs in a
  bridge-level UI design, not this adapter spec.
- Authentication on the POST endpoint. The bridge as a whole assumes
  LAN-only deployment (no auth on the settings UI either); this
  adapter inherits that assumption.

## Architecture

### Package layout

```
internal/adapters/url/
  adapter.go              # Adapter struct + Name/DisplayName/Fields/Start/Stop/Status/ApplyConfig/UIRoutes
  config.go               # URLConfig (TOML), Validate
  config_test.go
  play.go                 # POST /play handler: parse, validate, build SessionRequest, call Manager
  play_test.go
  routes.go               # UIRoutes() returning the two routes (POST /play, GET /panel)
  ui.go                   # html/template fragment(s) for the panel and form
  ui_test.go
  adapter_interface_test.go  # mirrors plex/adapter_interface_test.go: confirms interface conformance
```

The adapter is a *peer* of Plex, not a layer on top of it. It has no
dependency on `internal/adapters/plex`. Cross-adapter preemption flows
entirely through `core.Manager.StartSession`.

### Reuse of existing core API

No new method on `core.Manager` is needed. `core.SessionRequest`
(`internal/core/types.go:13`) already has every field the URL adapter
needs:

| Field | URL adapter value |
|---|---|
| `StreamURL` | The user-supplied URL, verbatim. |
| `InputHeaders` | `nil`. URL adapter has no upstream auth. |
| `SeekOffsetMs` | `0`. Fire-and-forget; no resume. |
| `SubtitleURL` / `SubtitlePath` / `SubtitleIndex` | empty. v1 has no subtitle handling. |
| `Capabilities` | `{CanSeek: false, CanPause: false}`. The cap-check at `manager.go:238`/`:389` returns an error to any caller invoking `Pause()`/`SeekTo()` against this session. URL adapter never calls them itself. (Note: a Plex *controller* still subscribed at preempt time will receive HTTP errors on its Pause/Seek commands until told the session has stopped — see §"Cross-adapter preemption / Plex precursor".) |
| `AdapterRef` | `"url:" + short-random-id` per session (8 hex chars from `crypto/rand`). Opaque to core; used only by the adapter to correlate `OnStop` callbacks. |
| `DirectPlay` | `false` always in v1. With `SeekOffsetMs = 0` always, ffmpeg's `-ss` is unused regardless, so manifest-vs-direct classification has no observable effect on the data plane. Setting to `false` is the conservative default. Heuristic detection of HLS/DASH is deferred to whichever follow-up reintroduces a seek surface. |
| `OnStop` | Closure that updates the adapter's status struct based on `reason`. Reasons `"eof"`, `"preempted"`, and `"stopped"` (the literal `Manager.Stop()` reason at `manager.go:382`) all set `state -> Stopped` and clear `lastError`. Any other non-empty reason sets `state -> Error` and `lastError -> reason`. Empty reason is treated as `"eof"`. |

### Lifecycle

- `Name() string`: returns `"url"`.
- `DisplayName() string`: returns `"URL"`.
- `Fields() []FieldDef`: returns the field definitions enumerated in
  §"Config schema".
- `DecodeConfig(raw, meta)`: standard `meta.PrimitiveDecode(raw, &cfg)`.
- `IsEnabled()`: returns `cfg.Enabled`.
- `Start(ctx)`: spawns no goroutines and performs no upstream
  registration. Sets internal state to `StateRunning` (matching Plex's
  convention at `plex/adapter.go:219`) and returns `nil`. For this
  adapter, `StateRunning` means "enabled and ready to accept POSTs,"
  not "background work in progress" — there is no background work.
  **This is the key abstraction probe.** If `adapters.Registry` or
  the UI server's mounting code assumes every adapter has *something*
  to start, that is a leak; this adapter surfaces it.
- `Stop()`: sets internal state to `StateStopped` and returns `nil`.
  Does not stop a mid-cast URL session (the data plane is owned by
  `core.Manager`, not by the adapter). See §"Operational edges /
  Disable while playing."
- `SetEnabled(bool)`: implements the optional `ui.EnableSetter`
  interface (`ui/adapter.go:180-182`). Mutates `cfg.Enabled` under the
  adapter's mutex so the toggle handler at
  `ui/adapter.go:handleAdapterToggle` can flip the flag without
  returning 500. Mandatory: without it the toggle endpoint
  `/ui/adapter/url/toggle` returns 500 and the panel cannot be
  enabled from the UI. Plex reference: `plex/adapter.go:475`.
- `Status() Status`: returns the adapter's mutex-guarded status struct
  (`State`, `LastError`, `Since`). The mutex is shared with `OnStop`'s
  mutator and with `SetEnabled` so all reads and writes are
  serialized. Updated by `Start` (`StateRunning`), `Stop`
  (`StateStopped`), the play handler on success (`StateRunning`),
  `OnStop` on session end (`StateStopped` for `"eof"` / `"preempted"`
  / `"stopped"`, `StateError` for other non-empty reasons), and
  `ApplyConfig` failures (`StateError`). `Validate` is pure per the
  `adapters.Validator` contract and never mutates state.
- `ApplyConfig(raw, meta) (ApplyScope, error)`: validates, computes
  per-field `ApplyScope`, takes max. See §"Config schema" for per-field
  scopes. The `enabled` toggle is handled by the registry / UI server
  via `Start`/`Stop` + `SetEnabled`, so its scope is `ScopeHotSwap` by
  convention (matches Plex).
- `Validate(raw, meta) error`: implements the optional
  `adapters.Validator` interface. Pure: type-checks every field via
  `meta.PrimitiveDecode`, returns `FieldErrors`. Does not mutate
  adapter state. (No range checks needed in v1 since the only field
  is `enabled` and bool decoding succeeds or it doesn't.)
- `UIRoutes() []Route`: returns the two routes (§"HTTP surface"). The
  adapter implements the optional `adapters.RouteProvider` interface.

### Data flow (single play)

```
operator types URL  →  POST /ui/adapter/url/play
                            │
                            ├─ parse URL (net/url): malformed → 400
                            ├─ scheme ∈ {http, https}: else → 400
                            ├─ build SessionRequest:
                            │    StreamURL    = input
                            │    Capabilities = {false, false}
                            │    DirectPlay   = false
                            │    AdapterRef   = "url:<id>"
                            │    OnStop       = func(reason) { adapter.markIdle(reason) }
                            │
                            └─ Manager.StartSession(req)
                                    │
                                    ├─ probeForStart (10s hardcoded; see §"Known limitations")
                                    │     timeout / probe error → returns error → handler 500
                                    ├─ preempts active session if any
                                    │     prior session's OnStop fires async with reason="preempted"
                                    │     (Plex adapter: see §"Cross-adapter preemption")
                                    └─ data plane spawns ffmpeg, plays to EOF or until preempted
                                          plane goroutine exit triggers OnStop("eof"|"error: ...")

handler returns:
  - htmx form submit (HX-Request: true): 202 + "panel" fragment showing new state
  - other clients (curl/scripts):        202 + {"adapter_ref":"url:<id>","state":"running"}
```

## Config schema

Section `[adapters.url]`. One TOML key:

| Key | Type | Default | Apply scope | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `enabled` | bool | `false` | `ScopeHotSwap` | Same convention as Plex; registry handles toggle via `Start`/`Stop` + `SetEnabled`. |

Validation: type-check via `meta.PrimitiveDecode`. No range checks
needed since `enabled` is bool.

The migration path from a config without `[adapters.url]` is a no-op:
`enabled = false` is the zero value, the adapter mounts no routes, the
section may be absent.

**Deferred config:**

- `probe_timeout_seconds` was considered for v1 but omitted because
  `Manager.probeForStart` (`manager.go:96-98`) hardcodes its 10-second
  timeout with no per-request override. Honoring a configurable
  timeout would require adding `ProbeTimeoutMs` to
  `core.SessionRequest` and threading it through `probeForStart` — a
  real change to the core API surface that contradicts this spec's
  "reuse only" thesis. The 10-second default is acceptable for v1;
  the follow-up adapter that needs longer (e.g., the planned RTSP
  extension, where negotiation is genuinely slow) is the right time
  to add that field.
- `crop_detect` was considered for v1 but omitted because crop
  detection is bridge-level, not per-adapter. `Manager.probeForStart`
  reads `m.bridge.Video.AspectMode == "auto"` (`manager.go:104`) to
  decide whether to run `ProbeCrop`. The bridge-level field already
  governs URL casts the same way it governs Plex casts; adding a
  per-adapter override would require threading a new flag through
  `SessionRequest` (same objection as `probe_timeout_seconds`). If
  per-adapter crop control is later wanted, it is a clean follow-up.

Both deferrals are listed under §"Known limitations".

## HTTP surface

Mounted under `/ui/adapter/url/` by `internal/uiserver/`.

### `POST /ui/adapter/url/play`

Accepts form-encoded bodies (from the htmx panel form) and JSON bodies
(from `curl` / scripts).

**Form-encoded body** (browser, htmx-driven):
```
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
HX-Request: true
url=https%3A%2F%2Fexample.com%2Fvideo.mp4
```

**JSON body** (anything else):
```
Content-Type: application/json
{"url": "https://example.com/video.mp4"}
```

The handler discriminates response shape on the `HX-Request` header,
not on `Content-Type`. htmx sets `HX-Request: true` on every request
it makes; no general-purpose HTTP client (curl, wget, Python's
`requests`, scripted tooling) sets it. This is more robust than
`Content-Type` because it describes *who is asking* (a browser DOM
that wants HTML fragments back) rather than *what was sent* (which is
what `Content-Type` actually means). A `curl` user who forgets
`-H "Content-Type: application/json"` and uses `-d` (form-encoded by
default) still gets a JSON response back, which is what they expect.

**Responses:**

| Outcome | Status | Body (HX-Request: true) | Body (otherwise) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Validation error (bad URL or scheme) | 400 | error fragment | `{"error":"<message>","field":"url"}` |
| `Manager.StartSession` returned error | 500 | error fragment | `{"error":"<message>"}` |
| Session started | 202 | panel fragment showing "playing <url>" | `{"adapter_ref":"url:<id>","state":"running","url":"<url>"}` |

500 (not 502) is used for all `Manager.StartSession` errors because
the failure modes are heterogeneous (probe timeout, MiSTer
unreachable, ffmpeg spawn failure, modeline resolution) and 500 is the
only honest answer that fits all of them. This matches Plex's
existing convention of "single status for `StartSession` failures"
even though Plex chose 400; the URL adapter chooses 500 because
`StartSession` failures are not the client's fault.

### `GET /ui/adapter/url/panel`

Returns the htmx fragment shown inside the URL adapter card on the
settings page: a text input bound to the play endpoint, a Play button,
and a status line ("Idle" / "Playing: \<url\>" / "Last error: \<msg\>").
The fragment uses `hx-trigger="every 5s"` self-refresh so the status
line stays current after EOF or preemption without operator action.

The form's `hx-post` target is `/ui/adapter/url/play`; success swaps
the panel fragment with the response body. This means the form is just
a client of the API — no separate code path.

## Cross-adapter preemption (the abstraction probe)

The contract being validated:

1. Plex is mid-cast. Plex's session was created via `sessionRequestFor`
   (`companion.go:138`), which sets `OnStop`.
2. URL `POST /play` arrives. Handler calls `Manager.StartSession(urlReq)`.
3. `Manager.startPlaneLocked` (`manager.go:114`) enters its preempt
   path: captures the prior `OnStop`, cancels the current data plane
   context, drops `Manager.mu`, awaits the plane goroutine via
   `prev.Done()`, re-acquires the lock, then calls
   `notifySessionStop(oldOnStop, "preempted")`. **`notifySessionStop`
   launches `OnStop` in a goroutine** (`manager.go:38-43`), so by the
   time `StartSession` returns and the URL session is the active one,
   Plex's `OnStop` may not have completed. Any state the precursor
   work in §"Plex precursor" needs (e.g., the prior session's media
   key, transcode-session ID) must be captured *inside the closure at
   request-construction time*, not read from `Manager.Status()` from
   inside `OnStop`.
4. Plex's `OnStop` runs, completing whatever cleanup Plex needs. See
   §"Plex precursor" for what that cleanup must include — current code
   does not do enough.
5. Manager probes the URL, starts a new plane for the URL session.
6. URL session ends (EOF, error, or another preempt). URL adapter's
   `OnStop` fires; status reflects.

### Plex precursor (verified findings, required before URL adapter ships)

Verified by reading `internal/adapters/plex/companion.go:138-150` and
`internal/adapters/plex/timeline.go`:

- ✓ Every Plex `core.SessionRequest` is constructed via
  `sessionRequestFor`, which sets `OnStop`. All call sites
  (`handlePlayMedia`, `restartFromPlayQueueItem`, `handleSeekTo`,
  `handleSetStreams`) go through this constructor.
- ✗ Plex's `OnStop` body (`companion.go:144-150`) calls only
  `StopTranscodeSession` against PMS. It does **not** clear the
  Companion's `lastPlay` (`companion.go` session-state field) and does
  **not** notify the timeline broker that the prior session is over.
- ✗ Consequence: after a URL adapter preempts a Plex session, the
  timeline broker's `statusFn` returns `Manager.Status()`, which now
  reflects the URL session (with `AdapterRef = "url:..."`). Plex
  controllers still subscribed to the timeline will receive timelines
  *describing the URL session*, not a "stopped" timeline for the prior
  Plex media. The Plex app will misreport.
- ✗ Consequence: Plex controllers continue sending Pause/Seek/Stop
  Companion commands while their (now-defunct) subscription is alive.
  Those commands hit the `core.Manager` cap-check (URL session has
  `Capabilities{false, false}`) and return HTTP errors. The controller
  surfaces error toasts.

**Required precursor work** in `internal/adapters/plex/`, before the
URL adapter ships:

1. Extend the closure in `sessionRequestFor` so that `OnStop`, in
   addition to calling `StopTranscodeSession`, captures the prior
   session's full `PlayMediaRequest` (or at minimum
   `MediaKey`/`Key`/`ContainerKey`/`TranscodeSessionID`) at
   construction time and:
   a. Clears the Companion's `lastPlay` after step b runs (order
      matters — see step b's broker-call dependency).
   b. Pushes one "stopped" timeline to all current subscribers using
      captured state, **not** by reading `lastPlay` or
      `Manager.Status()` (both of which are unreliable at this point).
      The current broker push primitive `broadcastStatusOnce`
      (`timeline.go:214`) reads `t.playContext()` internally for
      media-key enrichment, which is the bug — so the precursor work
      adds a sibling entry point `broadcastStoppedFor(st core.SessionStatus, play PlayMediaRequest)`
      that takes the captured `PlayMediaRequest` directly and bypasses
      the `playContext()` lookup. The closure calls
      `broadcastStoppedFor(stoppedStatus, capturedPlay)` with the
      synthesized `state=stopped` status.
   c. **Race note:** `RunBroadcastLoop` prunes subscribers older than
      `defaultSubscriberTTL` (`timeline.go:21`, 90s) on every tick.
      The OnStop goroutine is racing with that loop. "All current
      subscribers" means subscribers live at the moment
      `broadcastStoppedFor` acquires its read of the subscriber list
      — pruned subscribers are by definition not listening, so
      missing them is correct. Implementer should not try to "freeze"
      subscribers across the OnStop goroutine boundary.
2. Decide whether the timeline broker should additionally suppress
   automatic timeline pushes when `Manager.Status().AdapterRef` is not
   one of Plex's own refs. The simpler answer: rely on (1.b)'s
   one-shot stopped push to clear the controller's view, and let the
   broker go quiet (no push) until the next Plex `playMedia`. The
   Plex app does not require continuous timelines after `state=stopped`,
   and the broker's existing duplicate suppression at
   `timeline.go:256` will keep this self-quiescent. **This decision
   is a deferred optimization, not a v1 blocker.** Item 1.b alone is
   sufficient for correctness.
3. Add a unit test in `internal/adapters/plex/` that simulates a
   foreign `core.SessionRequest` preempting a Plex session and asserts
   the broker emits exactly one `state=stopped` timeline for the
   prior media key **during the preempt window** (i.e., between
   `OnStop` firing and the next operator-driven `playMedia`).

These three items are **scope additions to this URL-adapter work
package**, not pre-existing Plex bugs to be fixed elsewhere. They are
listed here because they are surfaced by the URL adapter's existence
and have no purpose without it. Other Plex consumers (a future
Jellyfin adapter, etc.) inherit the fix.

### Note on cap-check errors during preemption

Even with the precursor work above, there is a small window between
when the new (URL) session becomes `Manager.active` and when Plex's
`OnStop` goroutine runs and pushes the stopped timeline. Plex
controllers that send Pause/Seek/Stop commands during that window
will hit the URL session's cap-check and receive HTTP errors. This
window is bounded by the latency of Plex's `OnStop` body (a single
HTTP RTT to PMS for `StopTranscodeSession` plus the broker push) and
is deemed acceptable for v1 — the next timeline poll closes the
window.

## Error handling

| Failure | Effect |
|---|---|
| URL parse fails (`net/url` returns error, or empty after trimming) | 400, no core call. Adapter status unchanged. |
| Scheme outside `{http, https}` | 400. Error message names the scheme to make the v1 limitation explicit ("scheme not supported in v1: rtsp"). |
| `Manager.StartSession` returns error before the plane starts (probe failure, MiSTer unreachable, etc.) | 500 with error message. Adapter status → `StateError`, `LastError` set. |
| Session preempts and the plane exits with `reason="preempted"` | Adapter status → `StateStopped`. `LastError` cleared. |
| Bridge-wide `Manager.Stop()` fires `OnStop("stopped")` | Adapter status → `StateStopped`. `LastError` cleared. |
| Session ends with EOF | Adapter status → `StateStopped`. |
| Session ends with ffmpeg error | Adapter status → `StateError`, `LastError` = decoded reason. |
| Two POSTs arrive concurrently | Both call `Manager.StartSession`. The second preempts the first (preempt logic is the same as within Plex). Both handlers return 202; the loser's session has `OnStop("preempted")` fire effectively immediately. No special locking in the adapter. |

## Security posture

Inherited from the rest of the bridge: LAN-only deployment assumption,
no auth on the UI or this endpoint. SSRF is not actively mitigated —
any URL the bridge can reach (including LAN-only HTTP services) can be
fetched by ffmpeg if posted. This is acceptable for the documented
deployment model and is consistent with the existing settings UI.

If the bridge is later exposed beyond the LAN, the URL adapter's POST
endpoint is one of several surfaces that would need auth; it is not
the only one and not specifically harder than the others.

## Operational edges

### Disable while playing

Toggling `[adapters.url].enabled = false` via the settings UI returns
immediately and does **not** stop a mid-cast URL session. The plane
continues to EOF or until preempted. This matches Plex's behavior:
adapter `Stop()` tears down adapter-owned background work (Plex's
discovery responder, plex.tv heartbeat, timeline broker), not the
shared `core.Manager` data plane. To stop a live cast the operator
issues a bridge-wide stop or POSTs another URL.

### Bridge restart mid-session

Session state is in-process only. The bridge does not persist any
record of an active URL cast (`bridge.data_dir` only stores
`device_uuid` and Plex token). On bridge restart mid-cast the session
vanishes; the operator re-POSTs the URL if they want it back. This is
acceptable for fire-and-forget v1.

### Bridge config update mid-cast

`Manager.UpdateBridge(b)` (`manager.go:336-340`) is a public method
called from the bridge save path. If invoked during a URL cast, the
mutated bridge config takes effect on the next session probe (the
in-flight session keeps its existing config). This matches the Plex
adapter's behavior and is not URL-specific. No special handling
needed in the URL adapter.

### Live field-order flip

`Manager.SetInterlaceFieldOrder(order)` (`manager.go:306-322`) is the
hot-swap path documented as a flagship `ScopeHotSwap` demo (project
CLAUDE.md). URL casts honor live `tff`↔`bff` flips identically to
Plex casts because the path operates on `m.plane.SetFieldOrder`,
which is adapter-agnostic. No URL-adapter-specific work is required;
listed here so the implementer doesn't accidentally add a guard.

### Logging

Use `slog` consistent with the Plex adapter:

- `slog.Info("url cast started", "url", redacted, "ref", req.AdapterRef)`
  on success.
- `slog.Warn("url cast failed", "url", redacted, "err", err)` on
  `StartSession` error.
- `slog.Debug("url session ended", "ref", ref, "reason", reason)` from
  `OnStop`.

`redacted` is `u.Redacted()` (the standard library helper that strips
`user:pass@` from a parsed URL). URLs MAY contain credentials in the
authority component; the adapter MUST NOT log them in clear.

## Known limitations (carried as v1 acceptances)

- **Probe timeout is fixed at 10s.** `Manager.probeForStart` hardcodes
  this; honoring a per-adapter override would require adding
  `ProbeTimeoutMs` to `core.SessionRequest`. Deferred to whichever
  follow-up needs it (e.g., RTSP, where probe is genuinely slow).
- **Crop detection is bridge-level, not per-adapter.** URL casts
  honor the bridge's `aspect_mode = "auto"` setting identically to
  Plex casts. A per-adapter `crop_detect` override would require
  threading a new flag through `SessionRequest` (same objection as
  `probe_timeout_seconds`); deferred to whichever follow-up needs it.
- **`DirectPlay` is always false.** With `SeekOffsetMs = 0` always in
  v1, ffmpeg's `-ss` is unused and the value has no observable effect.
  Manifest detection is deferred to whichever follow-up reintroduces
  a seek surface.
- **Pause/Seek commands from a stale Plex controller subscription
  during the preempt window** receive HTTP errors. See §"Note on
  cap-check errors during preemption". Bounded; closes on next
  timeline poll.

## Testing strategy

### Functional tests (`internal/adapters/url/` — unit)

- `config_test.go`: `Validate` accepts good config, rejects type
  errors, returns `FieldErrors` with the right `Key`.
- `play_test.go`: URL parse rejects malformed input; scheme validation
  rejects rtsp/rtmp/file/anything not http/https; `OnStop` callback
  transitions adapter status correctly for `eof`, `preempted`,
  `stopped`, and `error: ...`; URL credentials are redacted in log
  output (verify with `slog`'s test handler).
- `adapter_interface_test.go`: confirms `*Adapter` satisfies
  `adapters.Adapter`, `adapters.RouteProvider`, `adapters.Validator`
  (mirror of `plex/adapter_interface_test.go`).
- `ui_test.go`: panel fragment renders for each state (idle, playing,
  error); form's `hx-post` target is correct; response shape switches
  on `HX-Request: true`.

### Functional tests (`tests/integration/`, `-tags=integration`)

Same harness as Plex integration tests — `cmd/fake-mister` for the UDP
endpoint, real ffmpeg + ffprobe.

- `TestURL_PlayDirectFile`: spin up `httptest.Server` serving a small
  test mp4, POST URL, assert blits arrive at fake-mister, assert
  session ends cleanly on EOF.
- `TestURL_RejectsBadScheme`: POST `file:///etc/passwd`, assert 400,
  assert no plane started (verify by inspecting `Manager.Status()`).
- `TestURL_ProbeTimeout`: POST a URL pointing at an `httptest.Server`
  whose handler `time.Sleep`s past the hardcoded 10s probe ceiling;
  assert 500 within `2 * probeTimeout` (~20s) to absorb CI scheduling
  variance, assert no plane.

### Boundary-validation tests (the abstraction probe)

These tests are the spec's primary purpose. Without them, the
"validates the core/adapter boundary" thesis is unproven.

- **`TestRegistry_AcceptsAdapterWithNoBackgroundWork`**: instantiate a
  `Registry`, register the URL adapter (whose `Start` returns nil and
  spawns no goroutines), bring up the UI server, assert the lifecycle
  completes without errors and the URL adapter's UIRoutes are mounted.
  Verifies `adapters.Registry` and `internal/uiserver/` do not assume
  every adapter has background work to start.
- **`TestURL_PreemptsPlex_TimelineReportsStopped`**: start a fake Plex
  play session via the existing Plex test harness (or a synthesised
  Companion request), subscribe a fake controller to the timeline
  broker, POST a URL, assert: (1) Plex's `OnStop` fires with
  `reason="preempted"`; (2) **during the preempt window** (after
  `OnStop` fires, before any subsequent `playMedia`) the broker emits
  exactly one `state=stopped` timeline addressed to the prior media
  key; (3) the URL session is the active one in `Manager.Status()`.
  Scoping (2) to the preempt window keeps the assertion robust if
  later test phases issue further `playMedia` calls. This test
  enforces the §"Plex precursor" contract.
- **`TestURL_CapCheckRejectsCrossAdapterPause`**: with a URL session
  active (after URL preempted Plex), call `Manager.Pause()` directly
  and assert it returns the cap-check error. This proves the cap
  check is the wall, not the data plane.

The functional tests prove "URL plays"; the boundary-validation tests
prove the spec's central claim that `core.Manager` is genuinely
adapter-agnostic.

## Out of scope for v1 (clean follow-ups)

- Saved bookmarks (`[adapters.url].bookmarks = [...]`) and a
  bookmark-list UI fragment.
- yt-dlp integration as a separate adapter (`adapters.ytdlp`) or as a
  toggle inside this adapter.
- `rtsp://` / `rtmp://` (security cameras, OBS local streams).
- `file://` with an `allowed_paths` allowlist.
- A bridge-level cross-adapter "Now Playing" sidebar panel.
- An auth layer for the bridge UI as a whole (would also cover the
  URL POST endpoint).

Each follow-up is a clean addition; none of them require structural
changes to what v1 ships here.

- `docs/specs/2026-04-19-mister-groovy-relay-design.md` §4.5 — adapter
  ordering rationale.
- `docs/specs/2026-04-20-settings-ui-design.md` §6 — adapter contract,
  apply-scope model, registry pattern, route mounting.
- `internal/core/types.go` — `SessionRequest` shape.
- `internal/core/manager.go` — `StartSession`, preempt logic,
  `Manager.mu` discipline.
- `internal/adapters/adapter.go` — `Adapter`, `Validator`,
  `RouteProvider` interfaces.
- `internal/adapters/plex/` — reference implementation of every
  contract this adapter implements.
